title: Harvesting and tanning a rabbit pelt
subtitle: Salt and alum tanning a rabbit skin at home.
excerpt: A rabbit killed for meat produces a usable pelt as a byproduct. Salt and alum tanning is the oldest and simplest method, nothing caustic, no specialist equipment, and a result that's soft, supple, and lasts years. Here's how to skin without cutting the pelt, how to salt-preserve it before tanning, and how to work it through the alum bath.
sourceNotes: Carla Emery, Encyclopedia of Country Living, rabbit and tanning chapters. Cobbett, Cottage Economy (1822), Project Gutenberg, rural crafts chapter.

glossaryTerms.definition: The skin of a rabbit or other small animal with the fur still attached.
Potassium aluminium sulphate, a mineral salt used in tanning to preserve and soften animal skins without the use of strong acids or bases.
Scraping the fat and membrane from the inner surface of a skin before tanning; a necessary step to allow the tanning solution to penetrate.

content.text: A rabbit
pelt
tanned with salt and
alum

takes three to four days and produces a soft, fur-on skin that can be used for trimming, small bags, or kept as a craft material. The process has three stages: skinning cleanly, salt-curing to preserve the skin while you work, and working it through the alum bath to tan.
Skinning without cutting the fur
Hang the rabbit by the back legs. Make a shallow cut around each back leg above the hock, cut through the skin only, not into the muscle.

Pull the skin down from the cuts toward the head, using your hands to peel it away from the carcass. Keep your cuts shallow where the skin is tight over the belly. The pelt should come off in one tube without cuts in the fur side.
Cut the head free and cut away the tail. The pelt is now inside out, the flesh side is outward.
Fleshing and salting
Stretch the pelt flesh-side out over a rounded board or a section of plastic pipe. Use
fleshing
: scrape off all fat and membrane with a blunt knife or wooden scraper. Fat left on the skin prevents tanning solution penetrating.

Rub coarse salt generously over the entire flesh side. Fold the pelt flesh-side in and leave it in a cool place for 24 hours. The salt draws moisture out and preserves the skin for up to a week before tanning.
The alum bath
Make a tanning solution: dissolve 200 g of alum in 1 litre of warm water. Add 50 g of salt and stir until clear.
Rinse the salt off the pelt and submerge it in the alum solution, fur side down. Weight it so it stays submerged.
Leave for 48 hours, agitating and turning once or twice a day.

Remove, rinse lightly, and begin the working phase while still damp: stretch the skin repeatedly in all directions by hand, and pull it over a rounded edge (a board corner or a beam) to work the fibres soft as it dries. Repeat every 20,30 minutes for two hours. A pelt allowed to dry without working becomes stiff.
Finishing

A tanned pelt is dry, flexible, and pale on the flesh side. Rub a small amount of neat's foot oil or vegetable oil into the flesh side once fully dry to keep it supple. Store flat in a dry place. A well-tanned rabbit skin keeps indefinitely.
